# Discussion

Six findings merit closer discussion.

**Hyperparameter tuning improves performance unevenly across pavement families.** Optuna tuning raised
flexible-pavement LightGBM $R^2$ from 0.333 (an untuned configuration without traffic/LTE imputation) to
0.414, but pooled-rigid $R^2$ fell slightly, from 0.254 to 0.220, under the same tuning procedure. This is a
genuine methodological finding rather than a reporting artifact: with only 179 rigid sections partitioned
across a 4-fold group cross-validation objective (roughly 35-45 sections per fold), the tuning objective
itself carries substantial variance, and Optuna can select hyperparameters (here, deeper trees,
$\text{max\_depth}=7$) that fit that noisy objective without generalizing better to the held-out split.
Rigid-pavement models built on comparably small LTPP samples would likely benefit from nested cross-validation
or a larger outer test set to mitigate this effect.

**Falling Weight Deflectometer load-transfer efficiency is a substantively useful addition to the feature
set.** Including it improves rigid-pavement accuracy modestly and, more importantly, gives the SHAP analysis a
direct mechanistic variable to attribute IRI to, rather than only the faulting symptom of joint-support loss.
Of the feature extensions considered in this study, incorporating LTE has the clearest grounding in pavement
engineering mechanics.

**Ablating traffic loading reframes its role from negligible to modest but genuine.** Excluding AADTT/KESAL
costs 0.019 $R^2$ for flexible pavements and 0.017 $R^2$ for pooled rigid pavements — well short of the
dominant role classical mechanistic-empirical formulations often assign traffic loading, but distinguishable
from zero. Because imputation rather than row deletion is used to handle its missingness, this comparison is
made on the full analysis sample rather than on a smaller traffic-complete subset that could itself be
non-representative.

**Forecasting from recent history and structural attribution are distinct tasks with different practical
uses, and both are reported.** The operational, lagged-IRI models reach $R^2$ up to 0.70 for flexible
pavements, since a section's own recent IRI trajectory is a strong predictor of its next reading. The two
model families serve different purposes: a pavement management system deciding when to re-inspect a section
benefits from the operational model, whereas an agency evaluating which design or maintenance treatment to
specify benefits from the structural model, which isolates the effect of distress and structural variables
independent of a section's own measurement history.

**Pooling outperforms splitting for rigid pavements at the present sample size**, an empirical rather than
assumed result (@sec-results) that directly addresses whether JPCP/JRCP and CRCP should be modeled jointly or
separately.

**Naive-split inflation scales with how much of the outcome the model fails to explain, not with panel
structure alone.** @sec-results reports a controlled comparison — identical tuned hyperparameters evaluated
under a naive and a group-aware split — across all four family/submodel configurations. The inflation ranges
from negligible for flexible pavements (0.417 vs. 0.414) to substantial for pooled rigid pavements (0.440 vs.
0.220) to extreme for the JPCP/JRCP-only submodel (0.355 vs. $-0.294$), tracking almost exactly the ordering
of how little genuine variance each structural feature set explains. This is a more specific and more useful
claim than "naive splits inflate accuracy": it predicts, and the data confirm, that the risk of leakage-driven
inflation is greatest precisely where a model's explanatory power is weakest — the situation in which a
naive split's ability to smuggle in section identity matters most. A weaker structural model is therefore not
only less accurate on its own terms; it is also more vulnerable to being mistaken for a stronger one under an
insufficiently careful evaluation protocol.

Beyond these six points, the gap between single-split and cross-validated $R^2$ (0.414 vs. 0.227 for flexible
pavements) illustrates that panel data with repeated section visits is particularly unforgiving of evaluation
protocols that do not account for its structure. This has implications beyond the present study: work using
LTPP or comparable longitudinal pavement datasets should report train/test splitting strategy in sufficient
detail for this source of optimism bias to be assessed by readers. Two further robustness checks (@sec-results)
support the structural model's headline numbers: a five-draw multiple-imputation ensemble reproduces the
single-imputation results to within 0.05 $R^2$ for every family, and results are essentially unchanged across
three modern gradient-boosting implementations, indicating that neither the imputation strategy nor the
specific choice of ensemble library is responsible for the reported accuracy levels.
